rationale_for_baseline_tier: A hypertensive emergency with acute end-organ damage: critically elevated blood pressure (>220 systolic on the home machine), new altered mental status (hypertensive encephalopathy), visual disturbance and a severe worst-ever headache that cannot exclude intracranial haemorrhage. This is an immediate threat to life requiring controlled intravenous BP lowering in a monitored setting — only T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is appropriate. The ambulance provides monitored transport and the hospital delivers titratable IV therapy; home oral treatment is contraindicated.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Critically high home BP with headache, visual change and new confusion — hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute end-organ (cerebral) damage from severe hypertension needs immediate monitored IV therapy; delay risks haemorrhagic stroke, seizures or renal failure.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Your blood pressure is dangerously high and it's already affecting your brain. I need you to call 000 for an ambulance right now — stay sitting, don't stand up, and don't take any extra blood pressure tablets at home.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Seizure or loss of consciousness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, while on the line to 000
escalation_edges.rationale: Seizure or collapse signals critical progression (encephalopathy or intracranial haemorrhage); airway protection and paramedic resuscitation are the priority.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he starts to fit or passes out, gently help him to the floor, roll him onto his side, don't put anything in his mouth, and tell the 000 operator straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden focal weakness, facial droop, slurred speech, or the sudden worst headache of his life
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests acute stroke or intracranial haemorrhage complicating the crisis — equally a 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one side of his face or body becomes weak, his speech slurs, or the headache suddenly becomes even more explosive, tell 000 immediately — these are signs of a stroke.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is present but highly distressed
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the spouse clear, calm, stepwise instructions: keep him seated and calm, unlock the door for paramedics, gather the ceased medication bottles, and stay on the line with 000. Simple concrete tasks reduce panic and improve the handover.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known hypertension with recently ceased antihypertensives
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce (after the emergency is handled) that the medication must be restarted only under medical supervision and taken consistently thereafter.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them this is a possible hypertensive emergency with confusion and a severe headache. Keep him sitting or semi-upright and calm — do NOT let him stand or walk, and do NOT give any extra or oral blood pressure tablets at home. Have the spouse unlock the front door and gather the medication bottles he stopped to show the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary with the BP control achieved, end-organ findings (CT head, renal function, ECG) and the revised antihypertensive plan — take these to the GP for medication review and adherence support.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a routine or same-day GP problem, or advising home management, instead of an emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated hypertensive encephalopathy progresses to intracranial haemorrhage, seizures, coma or irreversible renal failure and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to ED by self-transport rather than calling an ambulance — deterioration (seizure, collapse, stroke) en route without monitoring or the ability to intervene.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — T5 is correct.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the cerebral picture is hypertensive encephalopathy, an acute stroke or an intracranial haemorrhage cannot be resolved without CT.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Do not attempt to resolve it remotely — all three are time-critical and require the same immediate 000 escalation and hospital imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to gather more history, or attempting to lower the BP at home to 'see if the headache settles'.
